summary:
The Slavic Migration Period (c. 500–700 AD) was the rapid demographic expansion of Slavic-speaking peoples from their homeland in the Pripyat Marshes region (modern Belarus/Ukraine) into the vast territories vacated or depopulated by the [[event:2038|Germanic Migration Period]] and the Byzantine and Avar wars — populating most of Central and Eastern Europe from the Baltic to the Adriatic, from the Rhine to the Don River, within roughly two centuries. This expansion was one of the most consequential demographic events in European history, creating the Slavic-speaking world that covers roughly half of Europe today.

The Slavic expansion proceeded in multiple directions simultaneously: west into the Elbe-Saale region (later German-Slavic borderlands), south into the Balkans (crossing the Danube in large numbers after 580 AD, permanently Slavicizing much of the Balkan peninsula previously inhabited by Latin- and Greek-speaking populations), and east across the Eurasian steppe. The expansion was facilitated by the Avar Khaganate's military campaigns, which used Slavic infantry as auxiliary forces and cleared territories for Slavic settlement. Byzantine sources complained of continuous Slavic raiding and settlement in Illyricum and Thrace. Within two centuries, Slavic languages had replaced earlier languages across an enormous territory; only Greek (in Byzantine core regions), Romanian (which preserves Vulgar Latin in the Carpathian region), and Albanian (an ancient Balkan survivor) maintained continuity in Slavic-expansion zones.
